title: SEGMENT 15 — Combinations: building on the checkpoint foundation

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Combinations: building on the checkpoint foundation
If checkpoint inhibitors are the foundation, what gets built on top?
Almost everything.
Modern oncology, in cancers where checkpoint inhibitors work at all, is increasingly a combination game.
Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy.
Pembrolizumab plus anti-angiogenic.
Pembrolizumab plus radiation.
Pembrolizumab plus another checkpoint inhibitor.
Pembrolizumab plus a personalized vaccine.
The drug has become a platform, and the field is exploring every possible thing to combine with it.
Let me walk through the major combination categories.
Chemo plus checkpoint.
This is, by volume, the most important combination in current clinical practice.
For first-line non-small-cell lung cancer in patients with low or intermediate PD-L1, the standard of care is pembrolizumab plus platinum-based chemotherapy.
The chemotherapy kills tumor cells in a way that releases antigen and stimulates immune recognition; the checkpoint inhibitor amplifies and prolongs the resulting T-cell response.
The KEYNOTE-189 trial established this approach in non-squamous lung cancer in 2018, with median overall survival improvements of around eleven months over chemo alone.
Similar combinations are now standard in triple-negative breast cancer, small-cell lung cancer, esophageal cancer, gastric cancer, biliary tract cancer, and others.
Chemo-plus-checkpoint is, at this point, the workhorse regimen of modern oncology in many settings.
Radiation plus checkpoint.
Radiation, as I mentioned, can spark immune activation.
Combining local radiation with systemic checkpoint inhibitors has been tested extensively, with mixed but interesting results.
The PACIFIC trial, already discussed, established consolidation durvalumab after chemoradiation in stage III lung cancer as a standard of care.
In some smaller studies, radiation has produced abscopal effects when combined with checkpoint inhibitors — distant tumors regressing in addition to the irradiated one.
The mechanism is presumably immune-mediated, but it remains rare and unpredictable.
Active trials are exploring how to make abscopal effects more reliable.
Anti-angiogenic plus checkpoint.
Many tumors recruit aberrant blood vessels that not only feed the tumor but also exclude T cells.
Anti-angiogenic drugs — bevacizumab against VEGF, or VEGF-receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitors like axitinib, lenvatinib, cabozantinib — normalize the vasculature and improve T-cell access.
Combinations of pembrolizumab plus axitinib or lenvatinib are now standard in advanced renal cell carcinoma and endometrial cancer.
Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab is the standard front-line therapy for advanced hepatocellular carcinoma.
The principle is consistent: dismantle the tumor's vascular barrier, then release the immune brake.
Vaccine plus checkpoint.
This is a frontier I'll spend more time on in Segment 17.
The idea is that a personalized vaccine generates new T cells against tumor neoantigens, and the checkpoint inhibitor lets those new T cells do their work.
Preliminary data from melanoma trials of mRNA neoantigen vaccines plus pembrolizumab are extremely encouraging, and we are in the midst of large Phase 3 trials.
Targeted therapy plus checkpoint.
Drugs that block specific cancer-driving mutations — BRAF inhibitors in melanoma, EGFR inhibitors in lung cancer — sometimes synergize with checkpoint inhibitors.
Sometimes they don't.
The interactions are complex, and the combinations are an area of active investigation.
A few principles emerge from the combination story.
First, combinations are usually more toxic.
Every drug brings its own side-effect profile, and combinations stack them.
Combinations of chemo plus immunotherapy mean both chemo toxicities and immune toxicities; combinations of two checkpoint inhibitors mean more immune toxicities, sometimes catastrophically more.
The therapeutic ratio narrows.
Second, sequencing may matter as much as combination.
Giving drug A then drug B is not always the same as giving drug B then drug A, and the optimal sequence depends on biology that is often only partly understood.
Clinical trials testing sequencing questions are slow and expensive, and many sequences remain unstudied.
Third, the right combination is increasingly biomarker-driven.

A patient with high PD-L1 and high TMB might do well with checkpoint monotherapy; a patient with low PD-L1 might need chemotherapy added; a patient with specific resistance markers might benefit from a non-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor in combination.
The era of one-size-fits-all is fading.
The era of biomarker-driven combination selection is upon us.
Pembrolizumab and nivolumab will, almost certainly, end up in more combinations over the next decade.
The pipeline is enormous.
The drug class is the foundation; everything else is, in some sense, scaffolding to extend and refine the immune response it unleashes.
